Document [4] (Title: HIV Prevention -- Introduction) Targets and initiatives set by the United Nations (UN), the WHO, and individual countries' centers for disease control have fostered immense progress in scaling up testing and implementing antiretroviral therapy (ART. However, knowledge of HIV status, use of preventive services, and access to and retention in care remain suboptimal in the United States and worldwide, with large inequities between populations. The Joint UN Programme for HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS) has set "95-95-95" goals for 2025. 95% of all individuals with HIV will know their status 95% of all those who have HIV will receive antiretroviral therapy 95% of all people on ART will achieve viral suppression The projected impact of this will be fewer than 370,000 people acquiring HIV and fewer than 250,000 people dying from HIV in 2025 (see Image. Global Trends in people acquiring or dying from HIV).

Document [7] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Etiology) Preexposure prophylaxis is significantly less costly than the treatment of HIV infection, both in terms of duration of use and per dose. The PrEP regimen is prescribed to high-risk individuals and is taken daily until the risk factors diminish, whereas antiretroviral therapy is taken for life by those who acquire HIV. Further, antiretroviral therapy consists of multiple, generally expensive drug combinations versus simpler and less costly options available for PrEP. The WHO defined being at "substantial risk" as anyone with greater than a 3 per 100 person-years risk of acquiring HIV in the absence of PrEP, the level above which PrEP was found to be cost-saving. [41] Below this level, PrEP may still be cost-effective.
Document [8] (Title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment as Prevention) An overwhelming body of evidence supports the TasP approach, also known as "undetectable=untransmittable" or "U=U." This approach engages patients and providers to treat HIV for the patient's health and to reduce the risk of sexual transmission to others. Clinical data only support U=U for sexual transmission. Even with undetectable viral loads, HIV transmission still occurs perinatally and via breast milk, and TasP's effectiveness in preventing exposure through blood has not been determined.[NIH. HIV Antiretroviral Guidelines - TasP. 2019]

Document [12] (Title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring Following Initiation of Treatment) When treatment is initiated, the patient's HIV viral load should be evaluated in 2 to 4 weeks and no later than 8 weeks. The viral load can be rechecked every 4 to 8 weeks to ensure the levels decline. Virologic suppression to undetectable levels (defined as an HIV RNA level of <200 copies/mL) may take up to 24 weeks of continuous therapy. If the HIV RNA level has not declined by 2 log10 copies/mL within 12 weeks and adherence is confirmed, evaluation for resistance with genotype testing for the patient's regimen is recommended. Genotype resistance testing should also be obtained if virologic suppression is not achieved. [13] When viral suppression is established, the viral load should be monitored every 3 to 4 months. [7] An HIV RNA level that remains persistently below this lower limit of detection demonstrates sustained virologic suppression—the primary goal of HIV therapy. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " HIV Antiretroviral Therapy, " for more information.
Document [13] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Etiology) HIV is a retrovirus that replicates primarily in CD4+ T cells and macrophages, resulting in an illness characterized by an acute phase, followed by a prolonged, generally asymptomatic infection, and then leads to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome with progressive destruction of the immune system as CD4+ cells are depleted. [16] The HIV viral load is exceptionally high during the acute phase of illness but rapidly decreases at the start of the latent period and then increases as the disease progresses. Due to the effectiveness of antiretroviral therapy, HIV infection is now widely seen as a manageable chronic disease. However, despite significant advancements in controlling the virus, the disease remains a significant global health concern fed by and contributing to inequities. [17] Prevention measures, including PrEP, are critical to decreasing the burden on patients, communities, and healthcare systems.
Document [14] (Title: HIV and AIDS -- Prognosis) Without therapy, HIV infections are invariably fatal. However, effective ART with sustained virologic suppression dramatically improves the clinical outcomes for patients with HIV. According to a meta-analysis from 2017, life expectancy in high-income countries is estimated to be 43.3 years if ART begins at 20, and 32.2 years if ART begins at 35. In low- to middle-income countries, life expectancy is estimated to be 28.3 years and 25.6 years if ART starts at 20 and 35, respectively. In all regions, regardless of income, life expectancy after starting ART has improved, reflecting improvements in therapy and management such as improved ART regimens, earlier initiation of ART, and better socioeconomic and adherence support. [92]

A: yes

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706) HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.

Within this category, the fixed‑dose combination of abacavir / lamivudine is listed as a member, representing one of the therapeutic regimens used to treat HIV infection by providing synergistic antiviral activity and helping to prevent the development of drug resistance.
Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1216) Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. Tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, and stavudine are all members of this nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can achieve more effective suppression of HIV, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of resistance development.
Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135) HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

Document [6] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993) Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.

Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226) Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1878) HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a major route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdine‑mesylate and efavirenz have the potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. In contrast, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this immunosuppressive medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in both preventing HIV transmission and selecting safe therapeutic options for patients living with the virus.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227) Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 825) Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antivirals that target the HIV protease enzyme, thereby preventing viral maturation and replication. Key members of this class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which functions as a protease inhibitor and is employed in combination antiretroviral therapy for the treatment of HIV infection. By inhibiting protease activity, these agents help achieve viral suppression and improve clinical outcomes in patients living with HIV.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105) HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 90) HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. These drugs belong to three principal pharmacologic classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Understanding the transmission risk posed by shared‑needle use together with the preventive and therapeutic properties of these NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors, and their class‑specific mechanisms, informs comprehensive strategies for HIV prevention, combination antiretroviral therapy, resistance mitigation and optimal patient care.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455) Integrase inhibitors classified as anti‑infectives for systematic use comprise the antiretroviral agents dolutegravir and raltegravir, both of which are members of this drug class. These medications function by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA integration into the host genome and contributing to effective systemic treatment of HIV infection. Recognizing dolutegravir and raltegravir as members of the integrase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their shared mechanism of action within antiretroviral therapy.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2491) Fosamprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir are all classified as members of the protease inhibitor group of direct‑acting antivirals. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes that are essential for processing viral polyproteins, thereby interrupting the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV. Their shared categorization under the “protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals” class highlights a common mechanism of action and therapeutic use in antiviral regimens, emphasizing the importance of this drug class in managing viral infections.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1662) Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and elvitegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as integrase inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, these agents target the HIV integrase enzyme and are employed in systemic antiretroviral therapy to prevent viral DNA integration into host cells, thereby contributing to the management of HIV infection.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 366) The combination of abacavir, dolutegravir, and lamivudine is classified as a member of the group “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations,” indicating that this three‑drug regimen belongs to the category of combination antiretroviral therapies used to treat HIV infection.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666) Delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs constitute a group of antiretroviral agents that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a distinct non‑nucleoside site, thereby preventing viral RNA transcription and replication. By sharing this common mechanism of action, delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are used in combination regimens to treat HIV infection, offering an alternative to nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and contributing to the overall effectiveness of antiretroviral therapy.
